title: Long service leave
content:
Long service leave

An employee gets long service leave after a long period of working for the same employer.

About long service leave:
Most employees' entitlement to long service leave comes from long service leave laws in each STATE OR TERRITORY. These laws set out:
- how long an employee must work to get long service leave (e.g. after 7 years)
- how much long service leave the employee gets

In some states and territories, long-serving casuals (employed on a regular and systematic basis for at least 12 months) are eligible for long service leave.

State/Territory agencies:
- ACT: WorkSafe ACT
- NSW: NSW Industrial Relations
- NT: NT Government
- QLD: Queensland Industrial Relations
- SA: SafeWork SA
- TAS: WorkSafe Tasmania
- VIC: Workforce Inspectorate Victoria
- WA: Department of Local Government, Industry Regulation and Safety

Pre-modern awards:
The state/territory long service leave laws don't apply when there are long service leave entitlements in a federal pre-modern award that would have covered an employer and employees before 1 January 2010. In that case, the entitlement comes from the pre-modern award.

Enterprise agreements:
Enterprise agreements made from 1 January 2010 can include long service leave terms, including portable long service leave and cashing out long service leave.

Portable long service leave:
All Australian states and territories have legislation for portable long service leave in some industries:
- Building and construction
- Contract cleaning
- Community services
- Security
- Coal mining (national scheme)

Portable means the employee keeps their entitlement even if they move between different workplaces/employers in the same industry.

Portable schemes by state (Building & Construction):
- ACT: ACT Leave
- NSW: Long Service Corporation
- NT: NT Build
- QLD: QLeave
- SA: SA Portable Long Service Leave - Construction
- TAS: TasBuild
- VIC: LeavePlus
- WA: MyLeave - Construction Long Service WA

Source: Fair Work Act 2009 section 113
